# Variance, determinism & reliability

*Generated by Claude from the benchmark's repeated-measure experiment runs, and reviewed by the author.*

Three repeated-measure studies, reported separately from the headline leaderboard (which holds
single-config runs). **§1–2** probe **temp=0 non-determinism** on hosted APIs — provider routing
plus batch-variant kernels move results run to run (see `reproducibility.md` for the why) — putting
error bars on the headline and on the 5.1→5.2 comparison. **§3** is a **sampling-reliability** study:
gemini-3.1-pro run 3× over the full corpus, decomposing a stable aggregate into per-fixture
consistency (how often the *same* fixture lands the *same* verdict).

## Contents

- [1. glm-5.1 vs glm-5.2 — temp=0, 3 reps](#glm-comparison)
- [2. Run-to-run determinism — 25 fixtures, temp=0](#determinism)
  - [Where the flips concentrate](#where-the-flips-concentrate)
- [3. Sampling reliability — gemini-3.1-pro × 3 reps](#reliability)
  - [Which fixtures flip](#which-fixtures-flip)
  - [What changes when a fixture flips](#what-changes-on-a-flip)

<a id="glm-comparison"></a>

## 1. glm-5.1 vs glm-5.2 — full corpus × 3 reps (`short` × `topology_ports`)

Three full 125-fixture repetitions per model. FULL% per rep, then mean±std.

| model | rep FULL% (each of 3) | mean±std | min–max |
|---|---|---|---|
| glm-5.1 | 63.2, 62.4, 61.6 | **62.4±0.7** | 61.6–63.2 |
| glm-5.2 | 44.8, 48.8, 52.0 | **48.5±2.9** | 44.8–52.0 |

**glm-5.1 − glm-5.2 = +13.9 pts** on FULL, mean per-model std ≈ 1.8 pts → a real regression (gap exceeds the run-to-run spread). The newer 5.2 is **worse** here on clean structural design, not better.

<a id="determinism"></a>

## 2. Run-to-run determinism — 25 fixtures × 3 reps (`short`)

Same prompt, same temp=0, three repeats. *FULL% spread* = how much the headline score moves
between identical reps; *flip rate* = fraction of fixtures whose credit was **not identical**
across all three reps (the per-item churn the aggregate score hides).

| model | rep FULL% (3) | mean±std | flip rate (≠ across 3 reps) |
|---|---|---|---|
| deepseek-v4-pro | 32, 44, 40 | **38.7±5.0** | 13/25 (52%) |
| glm-5.1 | 52, 68, 60 | **60.0±6.5** | 10/25 (40%) |
| glm-5.2 | 52, 48, 44 | **48.0±3.3** | 13/25 (52%) |
| kimi-k2.6 | 60, 60, 56 | **58.7±1.9** | 11/25 (44%) |

Even at temp=0 the per-fixture credit flips on a large share of items between identical reps,
while the aggregate FULL% stays within a few points — so **treat sub-~6 pt leaderboard gaps as
noise** and prefer multi-rep means for any close call. This is the empirical basis for the
leaderboard's "treat gaps under ~6 pts as noise" caveat. (This ~6 pt floor is for comparing two
**single runs** — the leaderboard default; §1's tighter ±1.8 pt is the spread of one model's
**3-rep mean**, which is why a 13.9 pt gap between 3-rep means is already unambiguous there.)

<a id="where-the-flips-concentrate"></a>

### Where the flips concentrate

The flips are **not uniform** — they cluster on capability-boundary topologies. Across the 4 models above, counting how many flip each fixture (credit not identical across its 3 reps):

| fixture | models flipping (of 4) |
|---|---|
| 049_telescopic_cascode_ota_nmos | 4 |
| 089_telescopic_cascode_diff_out_nmos | 4 |
| 001_5t_ota_nmos | 3 |
| 027_nmos_mirror_1to4 | 3 |
| 043_diff_pair_nmos_diode_loads | 3 |
| 057_cascode_cs_amp_nmos | 3 |
| 051_folded_cascode_ota_nmos | 3 |
| 007_cascode_nmos_mirror_1to1 | 2 |

Only **2/25** fixtures were stable in every model; the rest churn. The most flip-prone are the medium-hard idioms — telescopic and folded cascode OTAs, cascode amps and mirrors, the 5T OTA — i.e. the topologies a model is *on the fence* about. Easy fixtures (always FULL) and out-of-reach ones (always NONE) are stable, so the ~50% per-item flip rate is the model wavering at its capability boundary, not random noise — which is why aggregates stay stable while items churn. **Caveat:** small sample (25 fixtures x 4 models); suggestive, not definitive.

<a id="reliability"></a>

## 3. Sampling reliability — gemini-3.1-pro, full corpus × 3 reps (`short` × `topology_ports`)

The corpus leader, run three times over all 125 fixtures. **Temperature = 1.0** here (Google's
recommended setting for Gemini 3; temp=0's *mean* is comparable, slightly higher (88.8 vs 85.3) — see
`reproducibility.md`). The question is sharper than §1–2: when you sample the same model three times,
how often does a given fixture land the *same* verdict?

| rep | FULL% |
|---|---|
| 1 | 84.8 |
| 2 | 87.2 |
| 3 | 84.0 |

**mean 85.3 ± 1.4** — the aggregate is stable. But that stability hides churn. Decomposing the 125
fixtures by **how many of the 3 reps scored FULL**:

| consistency | fixtures | reading |
|---|---|---|
| 3/3 FULL | 94 (75.2%) | reliably correct |
| 2/3 FULL | 13 (10.4%) | usually right, occasionally slips |
| 1/3 FULL | 12 (9.6%) | usually wrong, occasionally lucks in |
| 0/3 FULL | 6 (4.8%) | never right |

Three roll-ups of that table:

| metric | value | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **all@3** (FULL every rep) | **75.2%** | reliably correct |
| majority vote (FULL ≥ 2/3) | 85.6% | best-of-3 by majority |
| **pass@3** (FULL ≥ 1/3) | **95.2%** | *can* produce it at least once |

Three things this shows:

- **Majority vote (85.6%) ≈ single-rep mean (85.3%).** Taking best-of-3 by majority buys almost
  nothing: the model is right about the same *number* of fixtures each time, just not the same *set*.
- **A 20-point reliability gap** (pass@3 95.2% − all@3 75.2%). The model *can* produce a correct
  topology for 95% of fixtures but does so *reliably* — every time — for only 75%. That gap is the
  reliability problem: "generate once and trust it" silently fails on the unstable fifth.
- **25 of 125 fixtures (20%) flip** between FULL and not-FULL across identical samples (26/125 = 21%
  change credit at all). Same model, same prompt, same temperature — a coin-flippish outcome on a
  fifth of the corpus.

<a id="which-fixtures-flip"></a>

### Which fixtures flip

The 25 FULL-flippers split almost evenly into mostly-right and mostly-wrong:

- **2/3 FULL — usually right (13):** `014` diff-pair, `027` 1:4 mirror, `034` wide-swing cascode,
  `047` source-degen pair, `050` telescopic OTA, `053` Miller two-stage, `075` SF cap-load, `094`/`100`
  diff/non-inv amps, `103` resistive-CMFB, `107` sampling switch, `122` super-SF, `124` Widlar-pmos.
- **1/3 FULL — usually wrong (12):** `030`/`033` cascode/wide-swing mirrors, `052` folded OTA, `054`
  Miller-pmos, `079` CG-amp, `098` unity buffer, `102` two-stage no-comp (pmos), `113` diff ring-osc, `117`
  telescopic wide-swing, `120`/`125` LC oscillators, `121` super-SF-pmos.

The **6 never-FULL** (0/3) are out of reach even with three tries: `074` SF-driving-R, `101`
two-stage-no-comp (nmos), `112` diff-ring-osc, `118`/`119` triple-cascode, `123` Widlar. The **94 always-FULL**
are the canonical/simpler fixtures. As in §2, the churn lives at the **capability boundary** — the
medium-hard multi-device idioms (cascode/telescopic/folded OTAs, oscillators, super-source-followers,
Widlar/wide-swing mirrors), not the easy or the impossible ones. Symmetry partners flip *independently*
(`121` super-SF-pmos at 1/3 vs `122` super-SF-nmos at 2/3; `120`/`125` LC variants both flip), so the
model is re-deciding per call, not stably "knowing" a topology.

<a id="what-changes-on-a-flip"></a>

### What changes when a fixture flips

The flips span a spectrum — from a wholesale **architectural swap** down to a wobbling **peripheral**.

**Architectural swap — `103` resistive-CMFB diff amp (FULL / NONE / FULL across reps).** The reference
is the minimal form: a diff pair with PMOS loads whose gates tie to a common-mode node set by **two
sensing resistors** (`R1`/`R2` average the outputs and bias the loads) — 5 transistors + 2 resistors.
One sample reproduces exactly that (FULL). Another keeps the same diff pair and resistor sensing but
**bolts on a full active CMFB loop** — an error amplifier (`M5`–`M8` plus its own tail) that compares
the sensed common mode against a reference and drives the load gates (12 devices). That is a legitimate,
arguably *better*, CMFB — but a different circuit, so it grades NONE. The model isn't failing; it
resolves "resistive CMFB" as **passive resistor biasing** one sample and **resistor-sensed active
feedback** the next.

**Peripheral wobble — `053` two-stage Miller (FULL / DECORATED / FULL).** The milder end: the amplifier
core (NMOS diff pair → PMOS mirror load → PMOS common-source stage → NMOS load → Miller `Cc` + nulling
`R`) is identical and correct in every rep. Only the **bias branch** wobbles — a resistor-set diode
mirror (`RBIAS` + diode `M8`, matches → FULL) versus a bare self-diode with nothing setting its current
(breaks iso → DECORATED).

Either way the lesson holds: the model **knows the topology but re-decides an *underspecified* part** —
sometimes a peripheral, sometimes the entire feedback architecture — differently on each sample. For a
designer that is the dangerous failure mode: every run yields a plausible circuit, but not the *same*
one.
